Immediate danger routes away from matching. | | desired_peer_traits | Optional | Preference fit | User marks each as required or preferred. | | undesired_match_constraints | Optional | Safety and comfort | Includes "do not match with same department/class/cohort" if relevant. | | fallback_preference | Yes | Backup match behavior | Auto-offer backup, ask first, or resource referral. | | prior_match_feedback | Optional | Avoid repeated bad matches | Structured reasons, not gossip notes. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Navigator-Specific Fields
chunks.text: | Field | Required | Purpose | Notes | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | navigator_status | Yes | Pool eligibility | Applied, training, verified, active, paused, suspended, retired. | | training_status | Yes | Safety gate | Track modules and expiry. | | verification_status | Yes | Trust gate | Admin-approved, domain-verified, background-check-required, etc. | | topics_can_support | Yes | Match fit | Controlled taxonomy. | | topics_cannot_support | Yes | Safety | Hard exclusion. | | max_active_matches | Yes | Capacity | Default low for pilot. | | current_active_matches | Yes | Capacity gate | Updated automatically. | | response_sla | Yes | Engagement | Example: within 24 hours. | | debrief_preference | Optional | Burnout prevention | Admin or peer lead can support. | | escalation_acknowledged_at | Yes | Safety | Must know what to do for risk reports. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Data Not To Collect By Default
chunks.text: - Diagnoses or suspected diagnoses. - Medication or treatment details. - Therapy/counseling notes. - Exact trauma narratives. - Detailed crisis history. - Exact home address or precise live location. - Immigration status or legal status. - Financial account details. - Government IDs unless a program policy explicitly requires verification. - Protected attributes as required fields, except when legal/program review explicitly approves a narrow use.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Progressive Disclosure Model
chunks.text: Use four levels: - Required operational data: consent, role, eligibility, timezone, language, availability. - Helpful matching data: goals, modality, support style, topics. - Optional sensitive matching data: identity tags, lived experience, accessibility needs, boundaries. - Relationship disclosure: what each person chooses to reveal after both accept. The UI should show why each sensitive field is being asked: - "Use for matching only" - "Show to my match" - "Do not use this" - "Skip"

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Matching Goal
chunks.text: The matching engine should optimize for a safe, useful first conversation, not a perfect long-term relationship. The best first version is: - One primary match. - One backup match. - Clear explanation factors. - Fast rematch if no response or low confidence.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Hard Gates
chunks.text: Hard gates remove a candidate before scoring. | Gate | Applies to | Rationale | | --- | --- | --- | | Consent active | Everyone | No consent, no match. | | Role eligibility | Navigator | Only active navigators can be suggested. | | Training complete | Navigator | Peer support requires baseline role competence. | | Verification complete | Navigator | Prevent untrusted people entering the pool. | | Capacity available | Navigator | Prevent burnout and nonresponse. | | Blocklist and report holds | Both | Respect user safety and prior incidents. | | Age/legal constraints | Both | Program and legal compliance. | | Language required | Both | Basic comprehension and comfort. | | Modality required | Both | Do not suggest a video-only navigator to a chat-only seeker. | | Availability minimum | Both | Ensure a realistic first contact window. | | Topic exclusions | Navigator | Do not route topics a navigator cannot support. | | Crisis or immediate danger | Seeker | Matching pauses and routes to crisis resources/escalation. | | Conflict-of-interest constraints | Both | Same supervisor, same class, same team, known person, etc. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Soft Score Components
chunks.text: Initial scoring weights should be configurable by program. A conservative pilot default: | Component | Suggested weight | What it measures | | --- | ---: | --- | | Goal fit | 0.22 | Navigator supports the seeker's stated need. | | Availability overlap | 0.16 | First response is likely within desired time. | | Support style fit | 0.14 | Listening/planning/accountability/resource style alignment. | | Lived-context preference | 0.12 | Optional shared context, only when requested. | | Modality fit | 0.10 | Chat/phone/video/in-person fit beyond hard requirements. | | Language and communication comfort | 0.10 | Shared language and comfort level. | | Reliability | 0.08 | Prior response rate, follow-through, check-in quality. | | Load balance | 0.04 | Avoid repeatedly routing to the same high-demand navigators. | | Continuity or novelty | 0.02 | Prefer continuity when it worked, novelty when a rematch is requested. | | Exploration | 0.02 | Controlled learning for uncertain but eligible matches. | Never show numeric scores to users. Show explanation factors like: - "Available during your preferred evening window" - "Comfortable with first-generation student questions" - "Prefers practical next-step planning" - "Can chat within 24 hours"

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Sensitive Attribute Rules
chunks.text: - Sensitive attributes can support matching only when the user opts in. - Sensitive attributes should not be used to deny access to support. - Sensitive attributes should not appear in explanations unless the user explicitly chose to share them. - Fairness auditing can use sensitive attributes in aggregate, access-controlled analysis. - Admin tooling must separate "why this match was shown" from sensitive raw fields.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Matching Pipeline
chunks.text: - Load seeker context and eligible navigator pool. - Apply immediate-help and policy triage. - Apply hard gates. - Score remaining candidates. - Apply fairness and capacity constraints. - Pick primary, backup, and optional alternatives. - Generate user-safe explanation factors. - Create a suggestion record with policy version and feature snapshot. - Wait for seeker action. - Ask navigator to accept if mutual consent is required. - Create a relationship and first-contact plan. - Emit lifecycle events for learning.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Match States
chunks.text: | State | Meaning | | --- | --- | | suggested | Shown to seeker but not accepted. | | seeker_accepted | Seeker wants the match. | | navigator_pending | Navigator must opt in before connection. | | active | Both sides connected. | | no_response | First-contact SLA missed. | | backup_offered | Backup suggestion activated. | | paused | User or admin paused relationship. | | reported | Safety or conduct report exists. | | closed_success | Relationship ended normally. | | closed_rematch | User requested a new match. | | closed_admin | Admin ended it for policy/safety reasons. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Warm Introduction
chunks.text: The first contact should not be an empty chat room. Use a guided introduction: - Shared consent reminder. - Peer support scope. - Preferred names and pronouns only if user chose to share. - Conversation style preference. - Suggested opener based on goal. - Boundary reminder: no diagnosis, no emergency handling, no pressure to disclose. - Report, block, and rematch controls visible from the start.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: First Conversation Guide
chunks.text: Provide a small structured guide inspired by active listening models: - Acknowledge: "What would feel useful to talk through first?" - Support: "Would you rather be heard, brainstorm next steps, or find resources?" - Keep in touch: "Would you like to check in again, pause, or request another navigator?" This should be optional and subtle. Do not make the chat feel scripted.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Check-In Schedule
chunks.text: | Timing | Recipient | Purpose | | --- | --- | --- | | Immediately after match | Both | Confirm expectations and next step. | | 24 hours | Seeker | Did the navigator respond? Need backup? | | 24 hours | Navigator | Any concern, overload, or unclear situation? | | 72 hours | Both | First conversation quality and safety. | | Weekly while active | Both | Continue, pause, rematch, close, report. | | On closure | Both | Outcome, reason, and optional feedback. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: No-Response Handling
chunks.text: No-response should be designed as a normal branch, not a failure. - If navigator does not accept within SLA, offer backup automatically or ask seeker. - If accepted but no first message within SLA, prompt navigator once. - If still no response, pause the navigator's availability and route seeker to backup. - Record the event for reliability scoring. - If repeated, notify admin and reduce navigator capacity.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Rematch Handling
chunks.text: Users should not have to justify a rematch. Give optional structured reasons: - Not the right topic fit. - Not the right support style. - Schedule did not work. - I want a different shared background. - I want less shared background. - I know this person. - I felt uncomfortable. - Safety concern. - Other. Only safety concern and policy issues require admin review. Preference mismatch can silently improve future matching.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Safety Boundary
chunks.text: Peer-Navigator Network should be positioned as non-crisis peer support. If a user indicates immediate danger or crisis: - Stop matching flow. - Show emergency guidance and 988 in the United States. - If the deployment has trained staff or a partner escalation policy, trigger that policy. - Do not ask the peer navigator to handle crisis intervention unless they are explicitly trained, supervised, and operating inside that program.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Reports
chunks.text: Every active relationship needs: - Report concern. - Block user. - Request rematch. - Pause notifications. - Get crisis resources. Report categories: - I feel unsafe. - Harassment or discrimination. - Boundary violation. - Pressure to disclose. - Medical/legal/financial advice. - Crisis or self-harm concern. - Spam or impersonation. - Other conduct concern.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Admin Review Queue
chunks.text: Admin queue should include: - New navigator applications. - Training completion exceptions. - Verification failures. - Safety reports. - Repeated no-response navigators. - High-risk keywords or manually escalated check-ins if policy permits. - Fairness audit alerts. - Capacity and burnout flags.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Navigator Burnout Prevention
chunks.text: Navigator safety is part of user safety. - Set max active matches. - Let navigators pause instantly. - Remind navigators to avoid over-sharing. - Offer debrief after difficult conversations. - Track repeated high-intensity topics. - Rotate navigators away from exhausting queues. - Require retraining after reports or long inactivity.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Privacy, Security, And Compliance Plan
chunks.text: This is health-adjacent and potentially student-data-adjacent, so legal review is required before production launch. The product should be designed to reduce legal and privacy risk even before formal review.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Privacy Requirements
chunks.text: - Maintain a data inventory for every profile, matching, report, and engagement field. - Record purpose for collection and purpose for matching use. - Separate display profile from private matching profile. - Encrypt sensitive fields at rest where the stack supports it. - Restrict admin access by role. - Avoid third-party ad/tracking SDKs on peer support pages. - Provide export and deletion workflows where applicable. - Define retention windows before launch. - Redact sensitive fields from analytics logs. - Log match decisions without storing full sensitive raw text.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Suggested Retention Defaults
chunks.text: | Data | Retention suggestion | | --- | --- | | Consent records | Life of account plus policy-required audit window. | | Active profile fields | Until user deletes or account closes. | | Sensitive optional tags | Reconfirm every 6 months; delete on request. | | Match suggestions | 12 months for audit, then aggregate/anonymize. | | Engagement check-ins | 12 months, shorter if no safety issue. | | Safety reports | Legal/program review required; keep longer with restricted access. | | Raw free-text notes | Avoid when possible; if collected, shorter retention and restricted access. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Participant App
chunks.text: - /peer-navigator: entry and role selection. - /peer-navigator/find: seeker intake. - /peer-navigator/become-a-peer: navigator onboarding. - /peer-navigator/matches: current suggestions and active relationships. - /peer-navigator/resources: crisis and non-crisis resources.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Admin App
chunks.text: - /admin/peer-navigator/applications: navigator approval queue. - /admin/peer-navigator/training: training status and expirations. - /admin/peer-navigator/reports: safety and conduct reports. - /admin/peer-navigator/matches: match health and no-response queue. - /admin/peer-navigator/fairness: audit dashboard using existing fairness component. - /admin/peer-navigator/policies: matching weights, hard gates, capacity settings, consent version.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Data Model
chunks.text: Recommended entities: | Entity | Purpose | | --- | --- | | peer_participants | Account-level participant state and role intent. | | peer_profiles | Matching profile fields separated from auth identity. | | peer_privacy_settings | Per-field visibility and matching-use preferences. | | peer_availability_windows | Normalized weekly availability. | | peer_navigator_status | Training, verification, capacity, active/paused/suspended. | | peer_training_modules | Module definitions and versioning. | | peer_training_completions | Navigator progress and expiry. | | peer_match_preferences | Required/preferred/avoid criteria. | | peer_match_suggestions | Suggested primary/backup matches and explanation factors. | | peer_match_decisions | Accept/reject/rematch decisions. | | peer_relationships | Active or closed matched relationships. | | peer_relationship_checkins | 24-hour, 72-hour, weekly, and closure feedback. | | peer_safety_reports | Restricted report records. | | peer_matching_events | Event stream for learning and audit. | | peer_policy_versions | Matching, consent, retention, and safety policy snapshots. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: API Plan
chunks.text: The existing service contract document already proposes core endpoints. Extend it with network lifecycle endpoints. | Endpoint | Purpose | | --- | --- | | POST /api/peer-network/v1/intake | Save progressive profile intake. | | GET /api/peer-network/v1/profile | Read own profile and privacy settings. | | PATCH /api/peer-network/v1/profile | Update profile fields and visibility. | | POST /api/peer-network/v1/navigator/apply | Start navigator onboarding. | | POST /api/peer-network/v1/navigator/training-events | Record training progress. | | POST /api/peer-matching/v1/suggestions | Generate primary/backup suggestions. | | POST /api/peer-matching/v1/accept | Accept a suggestion. | | POST /api/peer-matching/v1/reject | Reject or request rematch. | | POST /api/peer-network/v1/relationships/:id/checkins | Capture lifecycle check-ins. | | POST /api/peer-network/v1/relationships/:id/report | File report and optional block. | | POST /api/peer-network/v1/relationships/:id/close | Close relationship with reason. | | GET /api/admin/peer-network/v1/queue | Admin applications, reports, no-response, flags. | | PATCH /api/admin/peer-network/v1/navigators/:id/status | Approve, pause, suspend, reactivate. | | GET /api/admin/peer-network/v1/audit | Fairness, safety, response, and quality metrics. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Phase 0: Governance And Product Rules
chunks.text: Goal: define the operating policy before code makes promises. Steps: - Define target population: students, alumni, employees, community members, or public users. - Define minimum age and jurisdiction constraints. - Define whether this is campus-affiliated, employer-affiliated, or standalone. - Write peer support scope and crisis disclaimer. - Write navigator code of conduct. - Decide verification level: email domain, admin approval, reference check, background check, or partner-managed status. - Decide whether mutual acceptance is required before revealing contact/chat. - Create retention policy and data inventory. - Create first matching policy version. - Define admin roles and access boundaries. Acceptance criteria: - Product scope is documented. - Consent copy has versioning. - Sensitive fields have purpose and retention. - Crisis handling policy is approved. - Navigator activation requirements are explicit.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Phase 1: Profile And Intake Foundation
chunks.text: Goal: replace demo-only selection with real intake primitives. Steps: - Add apps/frontend/src/lib/peer-network/types.ts. - Add schemas for participant, seeker profile, navigator profile, availability, preferences, and privacy visibility. - Add mock data provider for local development. - Refactor apps/frontend/src/app/peer-navigator/page.tsx into entry, scope, intake, and result states. - Replace "background only" matching with goals, availability, modality, style, and optional lived-context preferences. - Add "use for matching" and "show to match" controls for sensitive fields. - Add empty states when no match is available. - Add resource-only path for users who skip intake. Acceptance criteria: - A seeker can complete intake in under five minutes. - No sensitive identity field is required by default. - User can understand what data affects matching. - Demo peers are no longer the only model of the future product.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Phase 2: Matching Service Integration
chunks.text: Goal: connect intake contexts to the existing matching engine. Steps: - Create adapter from peer network profile to MatchParticipant. - Add hard gates for navigator status, training, capacity, language, modality, availability, blocks, and topic exclusions. - Add soft scoring weights by policy version. - Add primary and backup suggestion generation. - Generate user-safe explanation factors. - Add event recording for suggestion, accept, reject, timeout, rematch, and report. - Add unit tests for gates and scoring. - Add tests that sensitive fields do not leak into user-facing explanations. Acceptance criteria: - No inactive or untrained navigator can be suggested. - Suggestions include a primary, backup, explanation factors, and policy version. - Reject/rematch events improve next suggestion. - Unit tests cover hard gate failures.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Phase 3: Navigator Onboarding And Admin Review
chunks.text: Goal: make the supply side trustworthy. Steps: - Build navigator application flow. - Add training module definitions. - Add training completion state. - Add verification/admin approval queue. - Add capacity controls and pause/reactivate. - Add admin status actions: approve, request info, pause, suspend, retire. - Add expiry reminders for training. - Add navigator self-care and debrief prompt. Acceptance criteria: - A navigator cannot become active without required training and approval. - Admins can see why a navigator is blocked from the pool. - Navigators can pause themselves. - Capacity affects matching.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Phase 4: Engagement Lifecycle
chunks.text: Goal: make support continue after the match. Steps: - Add relationship entity and state machine. - Add warm intro screen. - Add first conversation guide. - Add 24-hour and 72-hour check-ins. - Add no-response timeout handling. - Add backup activation. - Add rematch flow. - Add closure feedback. - Add reliability updates for navigators based on lifecycle events. Acceptance criteria: - A user always has a next step after accepting a match. - No-response automatically routes to backup or asks the seeker. - Closure captures structured outcome data. - Navigator reliability updates are event-based, not manually guessed.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Phase 5: Safety, Reports, And Governance
chunks.text: Goal: add trust and safety operations. Steps: - Add report/block/rematch controls to active relationship screens. - Add safety report schema with restricted admin access. - Add escalation status: new, triaged, investigating, actioned, closed. - Add admin safety queue. - Add automatic holds for reported relationships. - Add navigator pause after serious report. - Add crisis resource surface reachable from every peer navigator screen. - Add audit log for admin actions. Acceptance criteria: - Users can report or block from any active relationship. - Reports create restricted records. - Serious reports remove a relationship from normal flow until reviewed. - Admin actions are auditable.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Phase 6: Fairness, Evaluation, And Scale
chunks.text: Goal: operate the network responsibly as it grows. Steps: - Connect outcome events to fairness audit dashboard. - Track exposure parity, acceptance parity, response parity, safety report parity, and quality parity. - Track navigator load and burnout risk. - Add policy simulation before changing matching weights. - Add A/B guardrails only after safety review. - Add privacy review before new sensitive fields. - Add periodic deletion/anonymization jobs. - Add operational dashboards: supply, demand, no-match reasons, wait times, first response, rematch reasons. Acceptance criteria: - Admin can see why users are not being matched. - Fairness metrics are available without exposing sensitive details broadly. - Policy changes are versioned. - The system can be audited from suggestion to outcome.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Header And Navigation
chunks.text: Because this repo supports turning features and pages on/off, Peer Navigator should avoid deep hidden navigation. Use: - A clear top-level "Peer Navigator" entry when enabled. - If disabled, remove it from primary navigation and direct old URLs to a helpful page not found/resource page. - On the Peer Navigator page, use tabs or segmented controls for "Find support", "Become a navigator", "My matches", and "Resources". - Hide "My matches" until a user has a relationship or signed-in state. - Use one primary action per screen. - Keep crisis resources visible but not visually alarming.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Match Card Requirements
chunks.text: A match card should show: - Name or pseudonym. - Navigator status: verified peer navigator. - Response expectation. - Shared fit factors. - Support style. - Availability overlap. - Primary action: "Request intro" or "Start with this navigator". - Secondary action: "See another option". - Safety links: "Report concern", "Get urgent help". Do not show: - Raw match score. - Sensitive fields the user did not consent to share. - Admin-only safety/training details. - Exact location. - Overconfident copy like "perfect match".

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Empty States
chunks.text: No-match states should be useful: - "No navigator is available for these exact preferences." - Offer broadened filters. - Offer waitlist. - Offer resources. - Offer to notify when a navigator becomes available. - Never blame the user for having constraints.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Product Health
chunks.text: - Intake completion rate. - Time to match suggestion. - Match acceptance rate. - Time to first navigator response. - First conversation completion rate. - 24-hour comfort score. - 72-hour helpfulness score. - Rematch rate and reasons. - No-match rate and reasons. - Resource-only path usage.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Safety Health
chunks.text: - Report rate per active relationship. - Serious report rate. - Median admin triage time. - Repeated navigator no-response rate. - Navigator pause/burnout rate. - Crisis resource routing count. - Block rate.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Fairness Health
chunks.text: - Suggestion exposure by opted-in demographic/lived-context groups. - Acceptance rate by group. - First response rate by group. - Helpfulness score by group. - No-match reasons by group. - Report outcomes by group. - Navigator load by group if relevant.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Navigator Health
chunks.text: - Active navigators. - Available capacity. - Utilization. - Response SLA adherence. - Training expiry. - Debrief requests. - Pauses and reactivations.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Unit Tests
chunks.text: - Hard gates exclude untrained navigators. - Hard gates exclude over-capacity navigators. - Hard gates respect blocklists. - Availability overlap works across timezones. - Required language/modality constraints work. - Sensitive fields do not appear in explanations without consent. - Backup suggestion is different from primary. - No-response updates reliability.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Integration Tests
chunks.text: - Seeker intake to suggestion. - Suggestion accept to relationship creation. - Navigator timeout to backup activation. - Report to admin queue and relationship hold. - Rematch to new suggestion. - Closure to outcome event.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: UX Tests
chunks.text: - Mobile intake does not feel like a long form. - User can skip optional sensitive fields. - User can find crisis resources within one interaction. - Match card explains why without exposing raw scores. - Empty state provides a useful next step.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Governance Tests
chunks.text: - Admin cannot see sensitive data without proper role. - Policy version is attached to each suggestion. - Deleted optional tags are no longer used in matching. - Audit log captures admin status changes.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Risk Register
chunks.text: | Risk | Why it matters | Mitigation | | --- | --- | --- | | Users mistake peer support for therapy | Could create unsafe reliance or misleading claims. | Strong scope copy, crisis routing, trained navigators, no clinical promises. | | Sensitive data overcollection | Identity and mental health data can harm users if leaked or misused. | Progressive disclosure, minimization, retention, restricted access. | | Bad match due to identity assumptions | Shared labels do not guarantee trust or fit. | Let users choose identity preference strength; include goals, style, availability. | | Navigator burnout | Support labor can be emotionally taxing. | Capacity limits, pause controls, debriefing, training, load balancing. | | Low supply creates no-match frustration | Early networks often have scarce navigators. | Waitlist, backup resources, broaden preferences, transparent no-match states. | | Bias in exposure or outcomes | Recommenders can under-serve subgroups. | Fairness audits, policy versioning, human review, avoid hidden sensitive use. | | Reports mishandled | Safety trust collapses quickly. | Restricted queue, SLA, escalation states, audit log. | | Legal uncertainty for schools | FERPA/HIPAA applicability depends on deployment context. | Legal review, no clinical notes, data inventory, partner-specific policy. | | Overconfident algorithm | Matching quality changes as network grows. | Explain factors, not scores; collect outcomes; run offline evaluation. | | Admin overload | Safety and verification queues can grow. | Queue prioritization, status filters, automation for low-risk tasks. |

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Immediate Engineering Backlog
chunks.text: Recommended first sprint: - Add apps/frontend/src/lib/peer-network/types.ts with participant, profile, preference, availability, navigator status, relationship, and report types. - Add apps/frontend/src/lib/peer-network/mock-data.ts with realistic seeker and navigator profiles. - Add apps/frontend/src/lib/peer-network/matching-adapter.ts to convert profiles into MatchParticipant records for the existing engine. - Refactor apps/frontend/src/app/peer-navigator/page.tsx from one-step demo to staged entry, scope, intake, suggestion, and resource states. - Add hard gates to the demo engine config for navigator status, capacity, modality, language, and topic exclusions. - Add explanation factor generation separate from scoring. - Add a no-match empty state. - Add a "Get urgent help" resource panel. - Add unit tests for matching gates and explanation redaction. Recommended second sprint: - Add route handlers for POST /api/peer-matching/v1/suggestions, /accept, /reject, and /events using the existing service contracts. - Add relationship state and check-in events. - Add navigator application and admin approval mock UI. - Connect fairness metrics to existing audit dashboard. - Add report/rematch/block controls to active match surfaces.

chunks.title: Peer-Navigator Network Implementation Plan
chunks.heading: Definition Of Done For MVP
chunks.text: MVP is ready for a private pilot when: - Seeker can complete consent and intake. - Navigator can apply, complete required training status, and be admin-approved. - Only active verified navigators are eligible. - Matching uses hard gates, soft scores, and backup suggestions. - Match cards show explanation factors, not raw scores. - User can accept, reject, rematch, report, or use resources. - No-response backup handling exists. - Admin can review navigator applications and safety reports. - Data inventory and retention policy exist. - Crisis scope and 988 resources are present for US users. - Basic fairness, response, rematch, and report metrics are visible. - Sensitive optional fields do not leak into explanations or broad admin views.

chunks.title: Practical Path 1: Stabilize Your Baseline (Sleep, Food, Movement, Breath)
chunks.heading: Why this matters right now
chunks.text: If your nervous system is overloaded, motivation tricks usually fail. The first move is not "try harder". The first move is to reduce physiological chaos so your brain can think clearly again. Global and clinical guidance consistently shows that mental health is shaped by daily risk and protective factors across environments, not one single event. That means small, repeatable behaviors can be protective when done consistently.

chunks.title: Practical Path 1: Stabilize Your Baseline (Sleep, Food, Movement, Breath)
chunks.heading: 1) Anchor wake time first
chunks.text: - Pick one wake-up time you can keep 6 to 7 days per week. - Keep variation within 60 minutes, even on weekends. - Get outdoor light within 30 minutes of waking when possible. Why: Stable sleep-wake rhythm improves emotional regulation and reduces stress reactivity.

chunks.title: Practical Path 1: Stabilize Your Baseline (Sleep, Food, Movement, Breath)
chunks.heading: 2) Add one predictable meal window
chunks.text: - If your eating is irregular, lock one reliable meal time each day. - Keep easy options ready: protein + complex carb + fruit. - Avoid long fasting when under high stress unless medically advised. Why: Energy instability amplifies anxiety and concentration problems.

chunks.title: Practical Path 1: Stabilize Your Baseline (Sleep, Food, Movement, Breath)
chunks.heading: 3) Move for 12 to 20 minutes daily
chunks.text: - Walk, stretch, stairs, bodyweight sets, or dance. - Choose intensity you can do even on bad days. - Track completion, not performance. Why: Regular movement improves mood, stress tolerance, and cognitive function.

chunks.title: Practical Path 1: Stabilize Your Baseline (Sleep, Food, Movement, Breath)
chunks.heading: 4) Use two-minute downshift breathing
chunks.text: Twice daily (morning and evening): - Inhale for 4 seconds. - Exhale for 6 to 8 seconds. - Continue for 2 minutes. Why: Longer exhalation supports parasympathetic down-regulation.

chunks.title: Practical Path 1: Stabilize Your Baseline (Sleep, Food, Movement, Breath)
chunks.heading: Minimal crisis guardrails
chunks.text: If you notice severe distress, hopelessness, or thoughts of self-harm: - Contact local emergency services immediately if in danger. - In the United States, call or text 988. - Reach out to a trusted adult, counselor, or health professional.

chunks.title: Practical Path 1: Stabilize Your Baseline (Sleep, Food, Movement, Breath)
chunks.heading: Your weekly scorecard
chunks.text: Track only these five items daily: - Wake time consistency - One stable meal window - Movement done - Two-minute breathing done - Sleep duration estimate If you hit 70% consistency, the week is a success.

chunks.title: Practical Path 1: Stabilize Your Baseline (Sleep, Food, Movement, Breath)
chunks.heading: Discussion prompts
chunks.text: - Which baseline habit gives you the fastest mental relief? - What makes sleep timing hardest in your current semester? - What is one "low-friction" movement option that works on your busiest day?

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: The myth to drop
chunks.text: "I should handle this alone" is one of the most expensive beliefs in student life. High performers are often the slowest to ask for help, which increases burnout risk. Resilience is strongly associated with social resources and quality support, not just individual toughness.

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: Layer 1: Daily peers
chunks.text: People you can text quickly for routine stress: - Study buddy - Roommate - Class friend Goal: low-stakes check-ins that prevent isolation drift.

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: Layer 2: Academic support
chunks.text: People and systems for course overload: - TA or professor office hours - Tutoring or writing center - Academic advisor Goal: solve academic friction early before it becomes identity stress.

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: Layer 3: Emotional support
chunks.text: People for mood and life pressure: - Campus counseling center - Mental health coach - Trusted mentor, faculty, or family member Goal: protect emotional stability during prolonged stress periods.

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: Layer 4: Urgent support
chunks.text: Immediate contacts for crisis situations: - Local emergency number - 988 (US) - Campus crisis hotline or emergency protocol Goal: remove decision fatigue when urgency is high.

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: The 15-minute setup
chunks.text: Complete this once this week: - Save all contacts with clear labels (for example: "Academic Help - Advisor"). - Draft one help message template you can send when overwhelmed. - Identify your first call for each layer.

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: Help message template (copy/paste)
chunks.text: "Hey, I am having a rough stretch and could use support with [class/stress/schedule]. I do not need a full solution right now, but can we do a 15-minute check-in?"

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: Common blockers and counter-moves
chunks.text: - "I do not want to burden people." -> Ask for a specific, time-limited check-in. - "I need to be sure it is serious first." -> Ask early, not late. - "I do not know what to say." -> Use the template above.

chunks.title: Practical Path 2: Build Your Support Map Before You Need It
chunks.heading: Discussion prompts
chunks.text: - Which support layer is strongest for you right now? - Which layer is missing and how can you build it this week? - What words make it easier for you to ask for help without shame?

chunks.title: Practical Path 3: A Study-Stress Operating System That Actually Works
chunks.heading: Why most study plans fail under pressure
chunks.text: Students often plan for ideal energy, then judge themselves during real-life stress. Resilient systems are designed for low-energy days, not perfect days.

chunks.title: Practical Path 3: A Study-Stress Operating System That Actually Works
chunks.heading: Part A: Weekly pressure map (20 minutes)
chunks.text: Every Sunday or Monday: - List deadlines, exams, and obligations for 14 days. - Mark tasks as high, medium, low consequence. - Choose one "must-win" task each day. Result: clarity reduces cognitive overload.

chunks.title: Practical Path 3: A Study-Stress Operating System That Actually Works
chunks.heading: Part B: 50-10 focus cycles
chunks.text: For deep work blocks: - 50 minutes focused work - 10 minutes reset (walk, water, breathe, no doom scroll) Repeat 2 to 3 cycles, then longer break. Why: structured cycles prevent depletion and increase consistency.

chunks.title: Practical Path 3: A Study-Stress Operating System That Actually Works
chunks.heading: Part C: Non-negotiable shutdown
chunks.text: Set a hard stop at night for at least 45 minutes before sleep: - no academic decisions - no inbox triage - no panic planning Use this window for recovery: shower, stretch, light reading, breathing. Why: recovery quality predicts next-day cognitive performance.

chunks.title: Practical Path 3: A Study-Stress Operating System That Actually Works
chunks.heading: Emergency protocol for bad days
chunks.text: When you are overloaded: - Do 10 minutes of task triage only. - Pick one 25-minute starter block on the highest-impact item. - Send one communication that reduces uncertainty (for example ask a clarifying question). This is a win day. Momentum beats perfection.

chunks.title: Practical Path 3: A Study-Stress Operating System That Actually Works
chunks.heading: Burnout early-warning checklist
chunks.text: If three or more appear for 2+ weeks, escalate support: - persistent sleep disruption - detachment or numbness - concentration collapse - increased irritability - frequent hopeless self-talk

chunks.title: Practical Path 3: A Study-Stress Operating System That Actually Works
chunks.heading: Discussion prompts
chunks.text: - Which part of the system helps most during exam weeks? - What is your biggest shutdown-window obstacle? - What would make your emergency protocol easier to trigger?

chunks.title: Practical Path 4: Bounce Back After Setbacks Without Losing Yourself
chunks.heading: Hour 0 to 6: regulate first
chunks.text: - Hydrate and eat something simple. - Move your body for 10 to 20 minutes. - Write one paragraph titled: "What happened (facts only)." Do not make life conclusions while dysregulated.

chunks.title: Practical Path 4: Bounce Back After Setbacks Without Losing Yourself
chunks.heading: Hour 6 to 24: run a clean review
chunks.text: Use this structure: - What was under my control? - What was outside my control? - What one system failure caused most of the damage? - What one change would prevent a repeat? Focus on process errors, not self-attack.

chunks.title: Practical Path 4: Bounce Back After Setbacks Without Losing Yourself
chunks.heading: Hour 24 to 48: execute one corrective action
chunks.text: Examples: - Book office hours. - Rebuild weekly study calendar. - Form a study partnership. - Move one high-risk task earlier each week. Confidence returns through action, not waiting.

chunks.title: Practical Path 4: Bounce Back After Setbacks Without Losing Yourself
chunks.heading: Anti-shame language to practice
chunks.text: Replace: - "I am a failure" -> "This strategy failed under these conditions." - "I always mess up" -> "I need a better system for peak weeks." - "I cannot do this" -> "I cannot do this with the current plan."

chunks.title: Practical Path 4: Bounce Back After Setbacks Without Losing Yourself
chunks.heading: When to seek professional support
chunks.text: If setback distress becomes persistent hopelessness, self-harm thoughts, severe sleep disruption, or loss of daily functioning, seek immediate professional or crisis support.

chunks.title: Practical Path 4: Bounce Back After Setbacks Without Losing Yourself
chunks.heading: Discussion prompts
chunks.text: - What is your most common rebound mistake after a setback? - Which reflection question gave you the best insight? - What is one corrective action you can complete in 24 hours?

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Why 90 days
chunks.text: Behavior change needs repetition across different stress contexts (normal weeks, exam pressure, and disruption weeks). A 90-day window is long enough to test what is real and short enough to execute.

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Pillar 1: Baseline health rhythm
chunks.text: Choose 2 non-negotiables: - stable wake time band - movement minimum - shutdown window before bed - consistent meal anchor Keep targets realistic. Consistency beats intensity.

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Pillar 2: Support architecture
chunks.text: Document your 4-layer support map: - peers - academic help - emotional support - urgent support Review monthly and update contacts.

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Pillar 3: Academic stress system
chunks.text: Set a recurring weekly planning session and define: - one must-win task per day - emergency protocol for overload days - post-exam rebound checklist

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Pillar 4: Meaning and motivation
chunks.text: Write a one-paragraph statement: - Why your education matters to you. - Who benefits when you stay steady. - What kind of person you are becoming. Purpose protects persistence during hard weeks.

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Day 60: stress test check
chunks.text: - How did your system perform during high pressure? - Which support layer did you actually use? - What needs simplification?

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Minimum viable resilience plan
chunks.text: If you feel overwhelmed, start here: - one sleep anchor - one support person - one weekly planning block Run that for two weeks. Then add one more layer.

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Discussion prompts
chunks.text: - What is your strongest pillar today? - Which pillar is missing and what is one next step? - What should every student include in a minimum viable resilience plan?

chunks.title: Practical Path 5: Build Your 90-Day Resilience Plan
chunks.heading: Evidence-informed references
chunks.text: - WHO mental health fact sheet: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/mental-health-strengthening-our-response - APA resilience topic: https://www.apa.org/topics/resilience - NIMH child and adolescent mental health: https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/child-and-adolescent-mental-health

chunks.title: Aether Home Content
chunks.heading: Aether Home Content
chunks.text: Privacy by Default Local-first processing, minimized data movement, and explicit consent patterns keep the product grounded in trust. AI supports reflection, triage, and navigation while preserving human oversight and clear boundaries. Keyboard-friendly, readable, responsive, and designed to work cleanly across phones and laptops. Check-ins, safety planning, care navigation, and habit-building patterns reflect real student wellbeing programs. Built for students The product focuses on stressful real-life moments like overwhelm, isolation, setbacks, and re-entry after hard weeks. Useful for campuses Aether shows how privacy-aware digital tools can complement counselors, peer leaders, mentors, and broader wellbeing programs. Clear to AI systems Canonicals, structured data, RSS, sitemap coverage, and an `llms.txt` endpoint make the site easier to interpret and cite accurately. What is Aether? Aether is a student resilience ecosystem that combines private reflection, peer support, guided pathways, and transparent AI governance. Who is Aether for? It is designed for students, campus wellbeing teams, mentors, and researchers who need practical, privacy-aware support experiences. What makes Aether different? Aether emphasizes privacy-first design, accessibility, evidence-informed workflows, and clear explanations of how AI is used. Where should someone start? Start with the resilience pathway for a guided overview, then explore Echo Chamber, Peer-Navigator, and the blog for deeper context.

chunks.title: Mentor Recognition Content
chunks.heading: Mentor Recognition Content
chunks.text: Mentor-informed product pivots Key architecture and UX decisions improved after mentor reviews. Feedback sessions incorporated Guidance translated into concrete accessibility, safety, and governance updates. Research and ethics checkpoints Mentor input strengthened evidence alignment and responsible AI boundaries. Clarity Under Pressure Mentors helped narrow broad ideas into practical, student-safe product pathways that teams can actually ship. Ethics Before Velocity Mentorship kept privacy, fairness, and duty-of-care considerations as non-negotiable guardrails. Research to Practice Mentors continuously translated theory into implementation details that survive real-world constraints. Critical reviews improved readability, accessibility, and the emotional tone of high-stress student journeys. Attribution by Design Major decisions are accompanied by mentorship context in internal changelogs, not just release notes. Mentor Credit Ledger Each milestone records who shaped the decision, what changed, and how the impact was measured. Every mentorship insight is converted into templates and guidance so future teams benefit too. Annual Gratitude Review A yearly review revisits mentor impact and updates recognition artifacts across docs and website. Why dedicate a full page to mentors? Because meaningful mentorship is not a footnote. It shaped the product strategy, safety model, and implementation quality of Aether. How should mentor names be displayed? Use explicit consent, preferred titles, and context about what each person contributed. Recognition should be accurate and respectful. Can this page support institutions and cohorts? Yes. The structure is designed to recognize individuals, faculty groups, and program cohorts with equal clarity. Mr. Shalif Shaoul Computer Science Teacher, PCSS II With gratitude for your guidance and encouragement throughout the Aether journey. Your mentorship strengthened both our technical direction and our confidence to keep building with purpose. Mr. Shalif Shaoul Computer Science Teacher Subasri Dorairaj & Ramanjanappa Ravikumar Parents and Lifelong Guides With deep gratitude for your unwavering support, guidance, sacrifices, and belief throughout the Aether journey. Your love, values, and steady encouragement made this work possible and gave it meaning beyond the project itself. Parent and Lifelong Guide Parent and Lifelong Guide Consistent direction during key architectural and product choices. Support that sustained momentum through complex and ambiguous milestones. Feedback that improved quality, clarity, and implementation discipline. Values-based coaching that reinforced privacy, safety, and fairness choices.

chunks.title: Aether Resilience Model
chunks.heading: Aether Resilience Model
chunks.text: Early Signal Detection Weekly check-in identifies stress, connection, sleep, and safety trends early. Students can create a practical safety plan and activate crisis support in one click. Resource navigator routes students to peer, self-guided, campus, or urgent options. Peer circles reduce isolation and create identity-safe belonging pathways. Habit planning builds long-term resilience through short, repeatable daily actions. The Jed Foundation School systems strengthening + youth suicide prevention pathway. Peer-led campus chapters and ASK model for early support. Helpline, local affiliate routing, education and support groups. Mental Health America Early screening and prevention resources at population scale. 24/7 free, confidential crisis response and implementation guidance. Multi-channel urgent support (call/text/chat) and accessibility options. WHO Mental Health Resilience framing with social determinants and systems-level response. Trainable coping skills and social support as key resilience drivers. NIMH Child and Adolescent Mental Health Early warning signs and prompt intervention reduce long-term harm. CDC Mental Health Population-level mental health education, stigma reduction, and data channels. Healthy Minds Network Campus mental health survey benchmarks and action toolkit. Institutional student health measurement and implementation toolkits. SOS Signs of Suicide Evidence-based student and staff training with risk reduction outcomes. Anonymous community-based support with moderated safety model. Anonymous journaling, mini-activities, moderated peer forum, and chat support. Sonar Mental Health Human-in-the-loop coaching with school escalation and care navigation. SilverCloud by Amwell Evidence-based digital CBT-style pathways and higher-ed support models. Large content library for stress, sleep, and guided practice adherence. Behavioral routines for stress and sleep with high daily engagement design. Anonymous emotional support and multilingual global access model. I felt overwhelmed by daily demands today. My sleep quality has made functioning difficult this week. Concentration problems disrupted my studies or work. I felt disconnected from people who matter to me. I felt stuck or hopeless about things improving. I know at least one trusted person I can reach out to. Urgent Crisis Support (988) Immediate support if you feel in danger or unable to stay safe. Call or Text 988 Peer Support Circle Speak with trained peer listeners in a low-stigma, non-judgmental setting.
